stories.title: Paul Cejas scores rezoning approval for Lincoln Road apartment project
stories.excerpt: Paul Cejas is advancing plans for a 212-unit mixed-use project near Miami Beach’s pedestrian promenade after city commissioners approved initial zoning amendments for the development.  Cejas, a former U.S. ambassador to Belgium under Bill Clinton, secured unanimous approval from the city for his development at 1600 Washington Avenue, near Lincoln Road, the Miami Herald reported. The development would require increasing the maximum building height from 100 to 150 feet.  Cejas first requested rezoning last year, when he proposed to replace the 15,300-square-foot commercial building on the property with a multifamily development, The Real Deal previously reported.  The apartments will span […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Paul Cejas rezoning approved for 212-unit mixed-use at 1600 Washington Ave, Miami Beach.
Height limit raised 100→150 ft; 36 studios, 144 1BR, 30 2BR units planned.
Second vote required September; project targets pedestrian-friendly Lincoln Road district.
stories.summary: Paul Cejas secured unanimous rezoning approval from Miami Beach city commission for a 212-unit mixed-use apartment development at 1600 Washington Avenue near Lincoln Road. The project includes 36 studios, 144 one-bedroom, and 30 two-bedroom units across 217,000 sq ft of residential space, plus 7,000 sq ft of retail. The city approved a height increase from 100 to 150 feet, though commissioners requested revisions to unit-size standards, retail design, and pedestrian areas before a September final vote.
stories.impact: Rezoning approval signals Miami Beach's continued appetite for walkable, mixed-use density near iconic retail corridors. This transaction may accelerate similar conversions of underperforming commercial sites to residential in South Florida's urban core, tightening already supply-constrained submarkets and supporting rent growth, particularly for studios and 1BRs on the Beach.

stories.title: Developers rack up incentives amid Fort Lauderdale affordability crunch
stories.excerpt: Developers are moving forward with two projects that would bring 210 apartments to Fort Lauderdale as the state grapples with an affordable housing shortage. McDowell Housing Partners is seeking a $640,000 opportunity loan from the City of Fort Lauderdale for a development planned through the state’s Live Local Act, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The law offers tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers who include below market-rate housing.  Miami-based developer MHP, led by William Patrick McDowell, filed an application for Ekos Melrose Manor, a 110-unit affordable apartment complex at 2790 West Broward Boulevard. The 1.2-acre site is under […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: McDowell Housing Partners seeks $640K low-interest city loan for 110-unit affordable at 2790 W Broward Blvd, Fort Lauderdale.
Live Local Act tax breaks unlock 80% AMI units; 22 1BR, 33 2BR, 55 3BR planned.
Housing Trust Group pursuing parallel 100-unit CRA-funded project; affordability incentives fuel 210-unit pipeline.
stories.summary: Two developers are advancing affordable housing projects in Fort Lauderdale totaling 210 apartments. McDowell Housing Partners is seeking a $640,000 opportunity loan from the city for Ekos Melrose Manor, a 110-unit complex targeting households earning up to 80% of AMI (Broward County AMI: $102,500). The project leverages Florida's Live Local Act tax incentives and qualifies for brownfield site benefits. Housing Trust Group is concurrently pursuing a second 100-unit affordable project with CRA funding and rezoning.
stories.impact: Fort Lauderdale's aggressive deployment of Live Local Act incentives and city/CRA capital for affordability signals municipal policy shift toward rental supply at lower rent points. These catalysts may reduce long-term displacement pressure and moderate rent growth in Broward, but also indicate developer perception that unsubsidized market-rate returns are insufficient in the submarket.

stories.title: Lefrak Offloads Brooklyn Rent-Stabilized Assets to HF NYC for $38 million
stories.excerpt: LeFrak has sold four rent-stabilized multifamily properties in southern Brooklyn for $38 million, Commercial Observer has learned. The developer offloaded more than 300 units in what is known as its Parkway Portfolio in Brooklyn’s Bensonhurst and Midwood neighborhoods to Avi Singer’s HF NYC, sources familiar with the deal told CO. J.P. Morgan Chase provided a $24.8 million acquisition loan to HF NYC for the purchase of the properties, located at 8301 and 7705 Bay Parkway in Bensonhurst and 1201 and 1640 Ocean Parkway in Midwood, according to Meridian Capital Group, which negotiated the debt. Meridian arranged the financing with a team led by Ben Himmel and Abe Hirsch. “We were glad to arrange this financing for HF NYC on a strong, well-located portfolio of rent-stabilized Brooklyn assets,” Himmel, vice president, debt capital markets, said in a statement. “[J.P. Morgan] Chase was highly engaged throughout the process, and our team worked closely with everyone involved to deliver a solution that met the sponsor’s objectives and supports the long-term stability of the portfolio.” Gary Barnett, chairman and co-CEO of Extell Development, is a personal investor in the deal, according to sources familiar with the transaction. Lefrak’s sale of the four rent-regulated assets occurred on the heels of New York City’s Rent Guidelines Board voted last week to freeze rents for the first time on one- and two-year leases in rent-stabilized buildings. Lefrak also sold a 166-unit market-rate multifamily building at 7410 and 7420 Ridge Boulevard in Bay Ridge, Brooklyn, to Hirsch last year for $24.4 million, CO previously reported. Lefrak, HF NYC, J.P. Morgan and Barnett did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: LeFrak sells 300+ rent-stabilized units in Bensonhurst/Midwood, Brooklyn to HF NYC for $38M.
J.P. Morgan provides $24.8M acquisition loan; deal includes 4 properties at Bay/Ocean Parkway.
Portfolio sale signals major owner exit from NYC stabilized housing amid regulatory headwinds.
stories.summary: LeFrak has sold its Parkway Portfolio—four rent-stabilized multifamily properties totaling 300+ units in Brooklyn's Bensonhurst and Midwood neighborhoods—to Avi Singer's HF NYC for $38 million. J.P. Morgan Chase provided $24.8 million in acquisition financing, negotiated by Meridian Capital Group. The properties are located at 8301 and 7705 Bay Parkway and 1201 and 1640 Ocean Parkway.
stories.impact: This exit by a major family developer from stabilized housing reflects structural profitability pressures in NYC rent-regulation regimes. The $38M valuation (~$126K per unit) for stabilized assets suggests cap rates that have compressed further below market-rate comps, signaling reduced development feasibility for new stabilized-inclusive projects and potentially accelerating conversions or dispositions of older stabilized portfolios across NYC.

stories.title: Prologis lands former Audacy site for $100M warehouse project
stories.excerpt: An affiliate of industrial real estate investment trust Prologis snagged a 25-acre parcel of land in the Chicago suburb of Glendale Heights, with plans to transform it into a pair of warehouses for $100 million.  The parcel sits at 375 Army Trail Road, and used to belong to New York-based broadcasting company Audacy, according to Crain’s. Audacy used the site to host a transmission tower for local AM radio stations WBBM and WSCR. Prologis is betting that record-low industrial space in the Chicagoland area signals a need for demand, and is moving forward with the $100 million plan for two […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Prologis acquires 25-acre former Audacy transmission site in Glendale Heights, Chicago for $100M.
Plans 454K sq ft dual-warehouse development; tight industrial supply justifies project feasibility.
Construction start planned later 2025; lower industrial availability in Chicagoland supports demand thesis.
stories.summary: Prologis acquired a 25-acre parcel in Glendale Heights, Illinois (375 Army Trail Road) from broadcaster Audacy's bankruptcy sale for $100 million, including acquisition and development costs. The REIT plans two warehouses totaling 454,000 square feet. Audacy's transmission operations are relocating to Des Plaines with Salem Media Group. Prologis cited record-low industrial space availability in Chicagoland and strong proximity to core markets as justification for the development, with construction expected to launch later in 2025.
stories.impact: Limited industrial supply in greater Chicago drives speculative warehouse development at significant capital outlay, compressing margins. For multifamily developers, industrial scarcity and rising land costs in Chicagoland may reduce available developable parcels and inflate acquisition pricing for mixed-use or TOD sites, constraining residential supply and supporting rents, particularly in submarkets near logistical nodes.

stories.title: Target Inks 135K-SF Lease at Queens’ Rego Park Shopping Center
stories.excerpt: Attention, Queens shoppers, you’re about to get another Target. The retail giant with over 2,000 stores in the U.S. is about to add another location in the busy borough of Queens. Target inked a 15-year, 135,000-square-foot lease at the Rego Park Shopping Center at 61-35 Junction Boulevard in Rego Park, property owner Alexander’s announced Monday. The Rego Park Shopping Center is a multilevel, 600,000-square-foot open-air mall anchored by wholesale giant Costco. The Target lease brings the property to 99 percent leased, according to Alexander’s, which is an affiliate and externally managed real estate investment trust (REIT) partly owned by Vornado Realty Trust. The asking rent for Target’s lease was not disclosed, but recent asking rents for retail space in Queens averaged $42.47 per square foot, according to data from CommercialCafe. The names of the brokers who worked on the deal were also unclear. Target and Alexander’s did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. Other retail tenants at the Rego Park Shopping Center include Burlington, Best Buy, Marshalls, T.J. Maxx, Aldi and Petco. Target — and the other mentioned retailers — will occupy space in what is known as Rego Park II. In May, Alexander’s sold its Rego Park I property, located at 96-05 Queens Boulevard, to Northwell Health for a total of $235.5 million. Northwell Health’s plans for that site are unclear. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Target signs 15-year, 135K sq ft lease at Rego Park Shopping Center, Queens; brings property to 99% leased.
Alexander's (Vornado affiliate) property now 99% leased; recent Queens retail rents ~$42.47/sq ft.
Rego Park anchors: Costco, Burlington, Best Buy, Marshalls, T.J. Maxx, Aldi, Petco, Target.
stories.summary: Target Corporation signed a 15-year lease for 135,000 square feet at Rego Park Shopping Center (61-35 Junction Boulevard, Queens), a 600,000-sq-ft open-air mall anchored by Costco. The lease brings the property to 99% occupancy with a weighted average lease term of 9.3 years. The asking rent was not disclosed, though recent Queens retail asking rents average $42.47 per square foot.
stories.impact: Major retailer commitment to Queens reinforces the submarket's retail demand trajectory and urban density. For multifamily developers, Target's expansion in Rego Park validates the area's foot traffic and residential density, supporting mixed-use and residential development yields in surrounding Queens neighborhoods; retail stability also de-risks ground-floor activation in new residential projects.

stories.title: CREFC’s Aidasani: Capital is Back but is Selective (VIDEO)
stories.excerpt: On the question of how wide the debt markets have opened up in 2026, Raj Aidasani, managing director, research with the CRE Finance Council, put the markets into perspective. “Capital is definitely available, but it is selective,” compared to the low-rate environment of 2021, when lenders were more active and more inclined to take risks. Today, Aidasani told Connect CRE at the CREFC Annual Conference, “they’re more focused on underwriting assumptions, property fundamentals, sponsor strength. They want to make sure those cash flows are durable.” Asked what the higher-for-longer interest rate environment as determined by the Federal Reserve means for commercial real estate borrowers and lenders, Aidasani pointed out that while the industry is affected by rates, “the Fed’s actions impact the Fed funds rate, which is an overnight rate. We care about the longer end of the curve.” In the video below, Aidasani provides additional insights on these questions, as well as on the office sector, which currently generates headlines about the sector’s recovery as well as ongoing distress. The post CREFC’s Aidasani: Capital is Back but is Selective (VIDEO) appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CRE Finance Council: Capital available but selective in 2026; lenders focus on cash-flow durability.
Fed rate hikes affect longer-end curve more than Fed funds rate; underwriting discipline tightens.
Higher-for-longer rates environment increases scrutiny of sponsor strength and property fundamentals.
stories.summary: Raj Aidasani, managing director of research at the CRE Finance Council, characterized 2026 debt markets as 'available but selective.' Lenders now prioritize durable cash flows, sponsor strength, and conservative underwriting assumptions rather than pursuing risk as they did in the 2021 low-rate environment. Aidasani noted that the Fed's actions on the overnight Fed funds rate matter less to CRE borrowers than longer-end curve movements.
stories.impact: Selective capital availability and stricter underwriting discipline directly compress development feasibility for marginal-return projects and increase equity requirements across multifamily. Developers with strong sponsor track records and proven NOI forecasts will outcompete weaker competitors; pro formas assuming ramp-up rents or speculative lease-up curves will face higher equity hurdles or rejected financing, potentially slowing starts in secondary markets.

stories.title: Citi Lends $45M on Luxury East Village Multifamily
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged a $44.5-million financing for 194 E. 2nd St., a 61-unit luxury family property with ground-floor retail located in Manhattan’s East Village. Managing director Michael Zaremski, senior director John Flynn and director Clayton Ross. worked on behalf of the borrower, Benchmark Real Estate Group, the New York-based company led by Jordan Vogel and Aaron Feldman. Citi provided the funding. “Demand for high-quality multifamily assets in Manhattan continues to be driven by exceptionally strong operating fundamentals, limited new supply and sustained renter demand,” said Zaremski. “194 E. 2nd St. represents a rare combination of institutional-quality amenities, durable cash flow and meaningful long-term value creation opportunities. “ Located at the corner of East 2nd Street and Avenue B, the six-story property contains 61 residential units and approximately 15,450 square feet of retail space. Benchmark acquired the property in 2024 and undertook a significant renovation program that included upgrades to residential units, common areas, lobby and amenity spaces. The post Citi Lends $45M on Luxury East Village Multifamily appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Citi provides $44.5M financing on 61-unit luxury East Village multifamily at 194 E. 2nd St, Manhattan.
Property includes 15,450 sq ft retail; Benchmark Real Estate Group owns after 2024 acquisition and renovation.
Strong Manhattan multifamily fundamentals cited: limited new supply, sustained renter demand, institutional amenities.
stories.summary: Citi provided $44.5 million in acquisition financing for 194 E. 2nd St., a 61-unit luxury multifamily property with approximately 15,450 square feet of ground-floor retail in Manhattan's East Village. Benchmark Real Estate Group acquired the property in 2024 and completed renovations to residential units, common areas, and lobby spaces. JLL Capital Markets arranged the deal, citing strong Manhattan multifamily operating fundamentals, limited new supply, and sustained renter demand.
stories.impact: Institutional lender appetite for Manhattan luxury multifamily with limited supply signals continued investor confidence in core urban rental demand. For developers, the transaction underscores that trophy-quality Manhattan assets with durable cash flows and retail components access abundant capital; secondary and tertiary markets face higher cost-of-capital, pressuring feasibility and potentially accelerating consolidation toward prime urban assets.

stories.title: Andee Robb Joins Marcus & Millichap to Lead Retail Leasing & Advisory Services
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced that Andee Robb has joined Marcus & Millichap as head of Retail Leasing & Advisory Services, a newly established role. Over more than 20 years in commercial real estate, Robb has held senior leadership positions with leading retail real estate organizations, most recently serving as VP of transaction management within JLL’s Retail Corporate Services group. “Andee will lead the development and expansion of our retail leasing business, partnering closely with our agents to identify new opportunities, strengthen retailer engagement, and evolve our leasing platform and service offerings to clients across North America,” said J.D. Parker, Marcus & Millichap’s EVP and COO. “Andee’s leadership, industry relationships, and strategic expertise will play a critical role as we expand our retail leasing capabilities.” Robb’s appointment marks Marcus & Millichap’s third major senior retail leadership hire this quarter. In May, the firm appointed longtime retail real estate specialist Michael Puline to the position of senior managing director and national director of retail. Earlier in June, the company named Thomas Gorman senior managing director and national net lease division leader. The post Andee Robb Joins Marcus & Millichap to Lead Retail Leasing & Advisory Services appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap hires Andee Robb as head of Retail Leasing & Advisory Services (new role).
Third senior retail hire in Q2 2025; Puline (national director retail) and Gorman (net lease leader) also appointed.
Firm expanding retail leasing platform and retailer engagement capabilities across North America.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap announced the appointment of Andee Robb (formerly VP, transaction management at JLL's Retail Corporate Services) as head of a newly created Retail Leasing & Advisory Services division. This is the firm's third major senior retail hire in the quarter, following appointments of Michael Puline as senior managing director and national director of retail, and Thomas Gorman as senior managing director and national net lease division leader.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, M&M's aggressive retail talent acquisition and platform expansion may reflect renewed broker confidence in retail leasing velocity and mixed-use development pipelines; this could indicate broader commercial real estate market stabilization, which indirectly supports construction credit availability and investor appetite for mixed-use residential-retail projects.

stories.title: Alexander’s Signs Target to 15-Year Lease in Rego Park
stories.excerpt: Alexander’s, Inc. has completed a 15-year lease with renewal options with Target Corporation at its Rego Park Shopping Center located on Junction Blvd at the Long Island Expressway. The lease spans 135,000 square feet. Rego Park Shopping Center is a multi-level, 600,000-square foot open-air shopping center anchored by Costco, Burlington, Best Buy, Marshalls, T.J. Maxx, Aldi and Petco. Including Target, the center is 99% leased with a weighted average lease term of approximately 9.3 years. The Target lease comes on the heels of the sale of Alexander’s Rego Park I property to Northwell Health for $235.5 million. The long-vacant retail property originally housed an Alexander’s department store. This past December, the REIT completed a $175-million refinancing of Rego Park Shopping Center. The interest-only loan is at SOFR plus 2.00% and matures in December 2030. The post Alexander’s Signs Target to 15-Year Lease in Rego Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Alexander's refinances Rego Park Shopping Center for $175M at SOFR+200bps, December 2030 maturity.
99% leased property; 9.3-year weighted average lease term; interest-only loan structure.
Parallel: Alexander's sold Rego Park I (former department store) to Northwell Health for $235.5M.
stories.summary: Alexander's completed a $175 million refinancing of Rego Park Shopping Center in December 2024 on an interest-only basis at SOFR plus 200 basis points, maturing December 2030. The 600,000-sq-ft shopping center is 99% leased with a 9.3-year weighted average lease term. Earlier, Alexander's sold its adjacent Rego Park I property (a vacant former department store) to Northwell Health for $235.5 million.
stories.impact: Strong retail leasing fundamentals and refinancing success in Queens indicate lender confidence in core urban retail corridors. For multifamily developers, Rego Park's high occupancy, long lease terms, and successful repositioning validate dense, mixed-use development in outer-borough nodes; the Northwell sale also signals healthcare/mixed-use conversion upside in former retail properties, opening adjacent sites for residential development.

stories.title: Rent Guidelines Board Vote Sends New York’s Landlords Into Uncharted Territory
stories.excerpt: With the recent 7-1 vote by the New York City Rent Guidelines Board (RGB) to freeze rents for both one- and two-year leases on rent-stabilized apartments in the city, two things seem likely. The first is that, from offering free child care to freezing the rents, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani seems determined to keep the promises he made on the campaign trail. The second is that lawsuits from angry landlords and landlord advocacy groups, who make the case that the city’s owners cannot maintain upkeep on rent-stabilized apartments without rent increases, are almost certain to follow, with Mamdani’s own promises one possible weapon in the legal arsenal against him. “This is a decision that was predetermined based on the mayor’s wishes, as he built the board to deliver what his wishes were,” said Kenny Burgos, a former New York State Assembly representative who currently serves as CEO of the New York Apartment Association, an advocacy group for landlords of affordable housing in New York City. Burgos is referring to Mamdani’s February appointment of six new members to the RGB, including new Chair Chantella Mitchell, a former executive director of the city’s Department of Housing Preservation and Development, following a string of resignations. “I’m confident that, under the leadership of Chantella Mitchell as chair, the board will take a clear-eyed look at the complex housing landscape and the realities facing our city’s 2 million rent-stabilized tenants, and help us move closer to a fairer, more affordable New York,” Mamdani said in a statement at the time. “At a moment when so many families are struggling to stay in their homes, this work could not be more important.” Burgos also points, as many surely will, to the resignation of RGB member Christina Smyth the morning of the vote. Smyth, an attorney for landlords and a landlord representative on the RGB who was appointed by then-Mayor Eric Adams, said in her resignation letter that the RGB had “stopped bein
bullets: NYC Rent Guidelines Board votes 7-1 to freeze rent-stabilized rents (0% increase, 1- and 2-year leases).
Freeze aligns with Mayor Zohran Mamdani campaign promises; landlord lawsuits expected.
Policy signals shift toward tenant protections; owner financial stress and maintenance pressures likely.
stories.summary: The New York City Rent Guidelines Board voted 7-1 to freeze rents for both one-year and two-year leases on rent-stabilized apartments, resulting in zero percent increases. The freeze aligns with campaign commitments by new Mayor Zohran Mamdani and follows his other tenant-friendly policies such as subsidized child care. Landlord advocacy groups and property owners are expected to file lawsuits challenging the freeze, citing inability to maintain properties without rent increases.
stories.impact: NYC rent-stabilization freeze reduces owner cash flows and deferred maintenance capital for a significant existing portfolio base, likely accelerating selective exits and consolidations. For new development, the policy further compresses return prospects on mixed-income stabilized components, likely steering developers away from stabilized-inclusive projects toward market-rate or 100% affordable (subsidy-driven) models, and reducing overall residential supply in existing regulated buildings.

stories.title: JPI closes on McKinney site for $113M mixed-income apartment project
stories.excerpt: JPI formally closed on the land required to develop a 393-apartment unit multifamily feature in McKinney called Jefferson Terry.  The project is set to be developed through a partnership with the McKinney Housing Authority, and will feature 177 units for those earning up to 80 percent of the suburb’s median income level, with another 20 for those earning 30 percent, according to the Dallas Business Journal. The joint venture is going to be called Jefferson Terry, and will be set across 16 acres at the corner of Highway 380 and Terry Lane. The total tab for the apartment complex is […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: JPI closes on 16-acre McKinney site for $113M mixed-income project; 393 units, ~$298K per unit.
177 units at 80% AMI, 20 units at 30% AMI; partnership with McKinney Housing Authority.
Deliveries start 2027, complete 2028; Class A amenities (resort pool, fitness, dog park).
stories.summary: JPI formally closed on a 16-acre site in McKinney, Texas (Highway 380 and Terry Lane) for a $113 million, 393-unit mixed-income development called Jefferson Terry, developed in partnership with the McKinney Housing Authority. The project includes 177 units for households earning up to 80% of area median income and 20 units for those at 30% AMI. Units range from one to three bedrooms (650–1,600 sq ft) with Class A amenities including resort-style pool, fitness center, and dog park. First deliveries expected 2027; full completion 2028.
stories.impact: JPI's continued dominance in North Texas (delivered 2,436 units in 2023) and housing authority partnerships demonstrate the scalability of public-private affordable models. For developers, McKinney's density bonuses and municipal affordability partnerships reduce project finance risk and attract institutional capital; this model is increasingly replicable across suburban Sun Belt markets, suggesting affordable mixed-income supply growth in job-rich suburban nodes, moderating rent escalation in outer rings.

stories.title: Tapas Concept Fat of the Land Joining Restaurant Collection at Centerview Office Complex
stories.excerpt: EMMES Realty Services and funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management, L.P. are expanding their collection of dining destinations at the Centerview office complex in Irvine with the addition of Fat of the Land, a tapas-driven concept from restaurateur Robert Paetz. The opening follows the success of its original Santa Ana location, which earned recognition on Yelp’s Top 100 Restaurants list. Located on the ground floor of Centerview (2010 Main, Ste. 170), the restaurant will further EMMES’ vision of creating a hospitality-forward environment that serves not only office tenants, but also the surrounding residential and business community. “We are incredibly excited to welcome Fat of the Land to the property and continue building a collection of restaurants that create energy, connection, and a true sense of place,” said Justin Nguyen, EVP of asset management for EMMES Realty Services. “Restaurants today are helping define how people experience a community, and Fat of the Land brings exactly the type of authentic, experience-driven hospitality we envisioned for Centerview.” The post Tapas Concept Fat of the Land Joining Restaurant Collection at Centerview Office Complex appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Fat of the Land tapas restaurant joins Centerview office complex, Irvine (ground floor, mixed-use activation).
EMMES Realty/Oaktree Capital expanding restaurant/hospitality collection; Santa Ana location earned Yelp Top 100.
Property focus: creating hospitality-forward community environment for office tenants and surrounding residents.
stories.summary: EMMES Realty Services and funds managed by Oaktree Capital Management announced the addition of Fat of the Land, a tapas-driven restaurant concept, to Centerview office complex (2010 Main, Irvine). The restaurant is relocating from its original Santa Ana location, which earned recognition on Yelp's Top 100 Restaurants list. EMMES is expanding its on-site hospitality collection to create a community-focused experience for office tenants and surrounding residents.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. However, office-anchored mixed-use hospitality expansion in Irvine may signal landlord repositioning of aging office stock toward lifestyle/amenity-driven communities. For multifamily developers, this indirect indicator of office-to-mixed-use conversion may justify ground-floor food-and-beverage planning in nearby residential projects targeting suburban office employees.

stories.title: BridgeCity Lends $72M on Long Island City Apartments Build
stories.excerpt: Brooklyn-based developer Elmord Management has landed $72.3 million of acquisition and construction financing to build a multifamily development at a former industrial site in Long Island City, Queens, Commercial Observer has learned. BridgeCity Capital originated the loan for the developer’s planned 161-unit project at 44-68 Vernon Boulevard in Hunters Point. Elmord acquired the property for $28 million from an undisclosed seller, according to BridgeCity. A warehouse-style structure built in 1953 is currently on the site. “This transaction is another example of our ability to structure and execute complex financing solutions efficiently, while continuing to support high-quality development projects across New York City,” EJ Ehrlich, director of originations for Brooklyn-based BridgeCity, said in a statement. The 44-68 Vernon property is steps from a warehouse property that was formerly planned as Amazon’s New York City headquarter before the e-commerce giant pulled out of the project in 2019. Elmford plans a 16-story, 142,697-square-foot apartment building that also includes commercial space. Elmord Management did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: BridgeCity Capital provides $72.3M acquisition/construction financing for 161-unit Long Island City project, Queens.
Elmord Management acquired 1953 warehouse site for $28M; 16-story, 142,697 sq ft planned at 44-68 Vernon Blvd.
Hunters Point location near failed Amazon HQ site; industrial-to-residential conversion on former warehouse.
stories.summary: BridgeCity Capital originated $72.3 million in acquisition and construction financing for developer Elmord Management's 161-unit multifamily project at 44-68 Vernon Boulevard in Long Island City (Hunters Point), Queens. Elmord acquired the property from an undisclosed seller for $28 million; the site currently holds a 1953 warehouse. The 16-story building will total 142,697 square feet. The property is located steps from the site originally planned as Amazon's New York City headquarters before the company withdrew in 2019.
stories.impact: Long Island City industrial-to-residential conversions remain capital-accessible despite high construction complexity, signaling sustained investor confidence in Queens multifamily demand. However, proximity to defunct Amazon HQ site indicates developers are repurposing underperforming industrial land in secondary neighborhoods; this conversion trend supports overall NYC supply growth but may moderate rent upside in Hunters Point relative to Manhattan/prime waterfront, compressing developer returns.

stories.title: JV Plans 4,000 Affordable Units in L.A., Starting With Former World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: A day before a major California housing bill goes into effect on July 1, two prominent commercial real estate firms announced a partnership to build thousands of new apartments in Los Angeles. Global investment firm Kennedy Wilson and Jamison, perhaps L.A.’s most active adaptive reuse firm, on Tuesday launched a joint venture with plans for 4,000 affordable housing units across the city. Specifically, the partnership is between Jamison’s new affordable housing subsidiary, Arden Residential, and Kennedy Wilson’s affordable housing development partnership, Vintage Housing. The new joint venture will start in August by converting the former L.A. World Trade Center in Downtown L.A. The 400,000-square-foot office complex at 350 South Figueroa Street will be converted to what the JV is calling Sky Castle, with 512 affordable housing units, including one-, two- and three-bedroom apartments. The first phase of the project will include changing the concourse levels into 241 units for families earning 30 percent to 80 percent of area median income. The second phase will convert the office tower above into 271 affordable units. California’s SB 79 goes into effect on July 1 to force city leaders to allow more multifamily development near mass transit stops, encouraging projects that can be up to nine stories high. However, L.A.’s lawmakers chose to delay the full implementation until 2030, and until then will allow new SB 79 projects to be only four stories on significantly fewer parcels. Jamison said it manages 18 million square feet of commercial office, retail, medical and multifamily properties throughout Southern California, and it has added more than 8,000 units since 2014, with an additional 2,000 units under construction. Kennedy Wilson has $36 billion of assets under management. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Kennedy Wilson and Jamison launch JV (Arden Residential/Vintage Housing) for 4,000 affordable units across Los Angeles.
First phase: 512-unit conversion of 400K sq ft former L.A. World Trade Center (350 S Figueroa), Downtown LA.
Project timing: announced day before major California housing bill takes effect July 1, 2025.
stories.summary: Kennedy Wilson (global investment firm) and Jamison (L.A. adaptive reuse specialist) announced a joint venture between Jamison's new Arden Residential subsidiary and Kennedy Wilson's Vintage Housing partnership to develop 4,000 affordable housing units across Los Angeles. The JV will launch in August by converting the former L.A. World Trade Center (350 South Figueroa Street, Downtown LA) into Sky Castle, a 512-unit affordable project featuring one-, two-, and three-bedroom apartments. The announcement came one day before a major California housing bill took effect July 1.
stories.impact: Large-scale affordable JV formation in Los Angeles signals institutional capital alignment with state/local affordability mandates and demonstrates adaptive reuse as scalable path to affordable supply. For multifamily developers nationally, this partnership model—pairing operational expertise (Jamison) with capital/financing capacity (Kennedy Wilson)—suggests a replicable template for unlocking affordable supply in high-cost metros; success could accelerate office-to-residential conversions and reduce development feasibility for market-rate projects lacking subsidy or policy tailwinds.

stories.title: Developers seek density bonuses to build apartments on Kendall resi lots
stories.excerpt: Two developers are eyeing sites near a Baptist Health hospital in Kendall for their separate three-story residential developments, which would tap density bonuses to bring a combined 146 units to the area.  The Serralta Family Trust, represented by Ignacio Serralta and Vivian Serralta, are planning 67 apartments for 8401 Southwest 94th Street, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The family’s primary residence currently occupies the 1.5-acre site. The Serraltas acquired it in 2000 for $312,000. According to a pre-application filed with the city, 10 percent of the development will be workforce housing.  Cape Summit 69 Holdings LLC, managed by Luis […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Two developers pursue density bonuses for 146 combined units on residential lots near Baptist Health, Kendall, Miami.
Serralta Family Trust planning 67 units (1.5 acres, 8401 SW 94th St); Cape Summit 69 planning 65→79 units (9202-9262 SW 84th Ave).
Both projects target 10% workforce housing; county rapid-transit corridor on Kendall Drive enables 36 units/acre density.
stories.summary: Two developers filed separate pre-applications for three-story residential developments in Kendall, Miami near Baptist Health hospital. The Serralta Family Trust is planning 67 apartments on a 1.5-acre primary residence site (8401 SW 94th Street), while Cape Summit 69 Holdings is planning 65 units (amended to 79 units) on a 1.5-acre vacant parcel (9202, 9232, and 9262 SW 84th Avenue). Both projects commit to 10% workforce housing and seek density bonuses enabled by proximity to Miami-Dade County's rapid-transit corridor on Kendall Drive, which allows 36 units per acre.
stories.impact: Density-bonus projects in Kendall reflect Miami-Dade's pro-density zoning near transit and workforce-housing policy tailwinds. For multifamily developers, this activity signals municipal appetite for mid-rise residential on underutilized residential land in suburban nodes; successful entitlements may validate similar adaptive-reuse strategies across Kendall and outer Miami-Dade, accelerating supply in job-proximate areas and supporting rents in the submarket.

stories.title: Isaac Toledano, Israeli investor’s dueling real estate lawsuits spill into family drama
stories.excerpt: Isaac Toledano is tied up in dueling lawsuits with an Israeli businessman over South Florida real estate in a dispute that has boiled over into family drama tied to one of Toledano’s daughters.  Yaakov Morad and his affiliate Apirion Real Estate sued Toledano on June 23, alleging that Toledano lied about the value of a 28,000-square-foot Aventura office building and then flipped it to Morad and Apirion at an “inflated” price, according to the complaint. Toledano used Morad’s limited English and the fact he was doing the deal as part of a 1031 exchange to pressure him through the due […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Isaac Toledano (BH Group) and Israeli investor Yaakov Morad in dueling lawsuits over South Florida real estate values.
Dispute centers on $28K sq ft Aventura office building flip and $720K+ capital-call default on Cutler Bay mixed-use project.
Toledano (major South Florida developer) and Morad relationship deteriorates; family/personal drama entangled with deals.
stories.summary: Israeli investor Yaakov Morad and his affiliate Apirion Real Estate sued Isaac Toledano on June 23, alleging Toledano misrepresented the value of a 28,000-sq-ft Aventura office building and flipped it at an inflated price. Toledano countersued the next day, claiming Morad failed to meet capital calls for the mixed-use Southland City Center project in Cutler Bay and is over $720,000 behind on funding obligations. The dispute has escalated into family drama involving Morad's brother's marriage to one of Toledano's daughters. Toledano, through BH Group (run with wife Liat), has become a prolific South Florida developer in partnership with the Pérez family's Related Group.
stories.impact: Minimal systemic impact. However, litigation between major South Florida developers and their capital partners signals deal-execution risks in the region's rising mixed-use pipeline. For other developers, the dispute underscores the importance of clear sponsor-capital alignment and capital-call structures in complex projects; project delays or financing gaps in the Southland City Center (Cutler Bay) could reduce competitive supply in Miami's mixed-use market if the project stalls.
